purpose: Provides a ranked hierarchy of observable indicators for detecting correlation regime transitions, enabling practitioners to monitor the most reliable signals and assess proximity to regime change.
components: Tier 1: IG credit spreads widening >75bp from trough (6/7 hit rate, 18-month lead)
Tier 2a: Credit-equity Merton basis convergence (5/7 resolution via credit widening)
Tier 2b: Yield curve un-inversion (currently active, but degraded by term premium dynamics)
Tier 3: CLO formation pace below $25B/quarter for two consecutive quarters
Supplementary: Fallen angel flow ($200-400B BBB- at risk), amendment activity (40-50% of leveraged loans), PIK toggle usage
current_assessment: Only Tier 2b is currently active, but its signal is degraded because ~75% of the un-inversion reflects term premium rather than growth expectations. Tier 1 (IG OAS) near cycle tights (~90-110bp) signals no imminent transition. CLO formation is slowing but not yet at the threshold. Overall assessment: early-to-mid accumulation phase, pre-Phase 2 in the Conundrum sequencing template.
limitations: Covenant-lite structures may extend Tier 1 lead time from 18 to 24-36 months
Tier 2b degraded by term premium dynamics — must use ACM decomposition to interpret
CLO formation indicator has only n=2 historical precedents (one contaminated)
All tiers calibrated on historical data with materially different market microstructure
Private credit opacity may delay all indicators

purpose: Provides a three-condition test for identifying when credit stress is sufficient to trigger a correlation regime flip toward demand-dominant/negative, producing a 60-70% probability of regime change when all three conditions are met simultaneously.
components: Condition 1: IG OAS widens to >165-185bp
Condition 2: HY-IG spread differential exceeds >350bp
Condition 3: Distressed ratio (HY bonds trading >1000bp over Treasuries) exceeds 10%
current_assessment: No conditions currently met. IG OAS at ~90-110bp (well below 165-185bp threshold). HY-IG differential within normal range. Distressed ratio at ~4-6% (elevated relative to overall tightness, confirming quality bifurcation, but below 10% threshold).
limitations: Calibrated on historical episodes with different credit market structures
Covenant-lite and private credit opacity may delay conditions being met
Thresholds may need recalibration if credit market structure has fundamentally changed
Does not specify time horizon for the 60-70% probability

purpose: Replaces the standard single-point correlation estimate with a probability-weighted bimodal distribution to capture the oscillating regime, enabling regime-aware portfolio construction and risk management.
components: Regime D (demand-dominant): 55-60% probability, negative correlation (-0.15 to -0.30 at 2Y, negative at 30Y)
Regime S (supply-dominant): 40-45% probability, positive correlation (+0.10 to +0.35 at 30Y, attenuated at 2Y)
Mean correlation: approximately -0.06 (misleading for both realized states)
Falsification thresholds: sustained >+0.35 for 6+ months falsifies oscillation; sustained <-0.20 for 6+ months falsifies positive-structural
current_assessment: Currently oscillating between regimes on a 3-6 month frequency. The bimodal structure makes a portfolio optimized for mean correlation suboptimal in both realized states. Barbell construction with convexity in both tails is mathematically dominant.
limitations: Probability weights (55-60% / 40-45%) are estimates, not empirically derived from a large sample
Falsification thresholds proposed but not yet tested
Framework does not predict transition timing
Fiscal conditioning and structural changes may shift the probability weights over time

purpose: Synthesizes multiple historical analogues to estimate the current phase of the correlation regime transition and project resolution sequencing, explicitly accounting for the ~30-40% genuine novelty in the current environment.
components: 1993-94 Bond Massacre: rate sensitivity and portfolio rebalancing dynamics
2004-06 Greenspan Conundrum: broken monetary transmission and discontinuous repricing (7.5/10 similarity)
1966-69 Great Inflation onset: fiscal dominance and Vietnam-era deficit dynamics
30-40% novelty discount for: passive investing (~50% AUM), basis trade leverage, AI wealth effects, private credit scale
Phase identification: accumulation (30 months) → contained deterioration (12 months) → discontinuous repricing (6 months)
current_assessment: Currently assessed at ~36+ months of accumulation, potentially entering or approaching the Phase 2 (contained deterioration) transition. The fiscal deficit conditioning thesis suggests the Phase 1-to-Phase 2 transition may be extended by ~3 years relative to monetary-only models. No exogenous forcing event has yet crystallized.
limitations: Each individual analogue is n=1 with materially different market microstructure
30-40% novelty estimate is a judgment call, not a calibrated figure
Passive investing extension effect is unverified (March 2020 contradicts the mechanism)
Specific vulnerabilities differ from all analogues (basis trade, private credit)

purpose: Uses the Kalecki-Levy accounting identity to trace the dual channel through which fiscal deficits simultaneously support corporate earnings and elevate term premium, revealing the paradox that fiscal austerity may be equity-positive at current deficit levels.
components: Earnings channel: ~6.5% deficit/GDP → ~$400-600B annual flow to aggregate corporate profits → ~$15-25 of ~$250-260 S&P EPS
Term premium channel: $2T+ annual Treasury issuance → 80-130bp adjusted term premium → higher discount rates
Quality differentiation: aggregate floor does NOT protect firm-level cash flow in leveraged credits
Austerity paradox: at current deficit levels, P/E expansion from lower term premium may dominate EPS decline from reduced fiscal transfer
current_assessment: Both channels are currently active. The earnings channel supports near-term equity performance while the term premium channel builds headwinds for duration-sensitive assets. The quality differentiation means credit vulnerability is building beneath the aggregate surface. The austerity paradox remains untested but analytically sound.
limitations: EPS attribution ($15-25) involves many intermediate assumptions; plausible range is $5-40
The austerity paradox depends on specific magnitude of P/E sensitivity to term premium
Aggregate identity says nothing about distribution across sectors or quality tiers
Fiscal path dependency creates circular reasoning risks (economy resilient because of deficits, deficits sustainable because economy resilient)

purpose: Decomposes the equity risk premium across three methods and two correlation regimes to reveal the conditional overvaluation of US equities and the cross-sectional bifurcation between mega-cap and the rest.
components: Method 1: Fed model spread at ~+30bp (lowest since 2007, critiqued for real/nominal comparison)
Method 2: DDM-implied ERP ~4.0-5.0% assuming bonds hedge
Method 3: Cross-sectional decomposition — mega-cap implied ERP ~2.0-3.0% vs. S&P 493 at ~5.0-6.5%
Regime conditioning: under positive correlation, required ERP rises 50-100bp → fair value ~4,650-4,900 vs. current ~5,500-5,700 (12-18% overvaluation)
Under negative correlation: approximately fair
current_assessment: The cross-sectional ERP decomposition reveals a market priced for negative correlation regime persistence and mega-cap dominance. The S&P 493 implied ERP of 5.0-6.5% is consistent with positive correlation pricing while the mega-cap 2.0-3.0% is consistent with negative. The index-level measure masks this bifurcation.
limitations: Entirely conditional on which correlation regime prevails
Fed model component conflates real and nominal quantities (Asness 2003)
Cross-sectional decomposition has not been empirically verified against actual cap-weighted vs. equal-weight correlation data
Mega-cap concentration makes the index-level ERP an average of two very different risk premiums
